repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem
Expected text for interactive marks is in a separate json file from the tutor file. When the tutor file is updated, line numbers (potentially many) have to be updated in the json file. This is a burden for maintenance and automatic testing.

## Solution

Put the expected text inline in the tutor file.
`This is wrong. [[This is right]]`
Parse and remove the comments before opening the tutor file so extmarks can be applied.

AI-assisted: Codex
Closes #38527

comment_data.body: I don't understand why there is `tostring` here
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -16,6 +16,28 @@ local tutor_hl_ns = vim.api.nvim_create_namespace('nvim.tutor.hl')
 
 local M = {}
 
+function M.load_metadata()
+  ---@type table<string, string|-1>
+  local data = {}
+  local lines = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines(0, 0, -1, false)
+
+  for i, line in ipairs(lines) do
+    local text, expected_text = line:match('^(.-)%s==(.-)==$')
+
+    if text then
+      if expected_text == '-1' then
+        data[tostring(i)] = -1

comment_data.body:
[`ipairs`](https://www.lua.org/manual/5.1/manual.html#pdf-ipairs) returns an integer for `i` and the `expect` table wants `table<string, string|-1>`

`expect` field is defined just above this block on line 2 of the file
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -16,6 +16,28 @@ local tutor_hl_ns = vim.api.nvim_create_namespace('nvim.tutor.hl')
 
 local M = {}
 
+function M.load_metadata()
+  ---@type table<string, string|-1>
+  local data = {}
+  local lines = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines(0, 0, -1, false)
+
+  for i, line in ipairs(lines) do
+    local text, expected_text = line:match('^(.-)%s==(.-)==$')
+
+    if text then
+      if expected_text == '-1' then
+        data[tostring(i)] = -1

comment_data.body: Because the JSON file (have to) use string for line number (e.g `"2": "this is correct"`), as JSON does not permit a number as key in a dictionary. Now that we get rid of JSON file, I believe we can just use number (instead of string) for line number.

comment_data.body:
why was the `<==` syntax chosen? if there is no particular reason, maybe using `[[...]]` is more readable, and also more familiar from Lua.
```suggestion
1)  Roses are red, [[1)  Roses are red,]]
2)  Mud is fun, [[]]
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -306,13 +306,13 @@ it would be easier to simply type two d's to delete a line.
 
  4. Type `2dd`{normal} to delete two lines.
 
-1)  Roses are red,
-2)  Mud is fun,
-3)  Violets are blue,
-4)  I have a car,
-5)  Clocks tell time,
-6)  Sugar is sweet
-7)  And so are you.
+1)  Roses are red, <==1)  Roses are red,==>
+2)  Mud is fun, <====>

comment_data.body: Originally I suggested `<-- -->` because it is Markdown syntax for comments. But no big deal whatever will be chosen

comment_data.body:
Yeah that is why I went with `<== ==>`. I changed to `=` so that the `-1` will be more readable.

But I like `[[]]` more so I will probably go with that. I think it does mean that the lua pattern will have to be escaped with something like `%[` though
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -306,13 +306,13 @@ it would be easier to simply type two d's to delete a line.
 
  4. Type `2dd`{normal} to delete two lines.
 
-1)  Roses are red,
-2)  Mud is fun,
-3)  Violets are blue,
-4)  I have a car,
-5)  Clocks tell time,

comment_data.body:
I think we should remove the `-1` pattern. In JSON file there is difference between "-1 as a number" and "-1 as a string", so we can differentiate "this line is always correct no matter its content" and "this line must be modified to '-1'".

I proposed the syntax `--->` for "this line is always correct thing", it is highlighted by legacy syntax, but I think that is good enough. I don't think we need "interactive feature" in stuff that is not actually interactive

https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/89b5a1a181538ee65b88a5b1412901e84f0eaa07/runtime/tutor/en/vim-01-beginner.tutor#L381

comment_data.body:
I agree that we should get rid of the -1 pattern. I'm not sure I understand what you're proposing with the `--->`, you're saying that it will conflict with the example you linked there?

Anyways, is this something we could explore in a followup PR instead of here?
